findings.fix_prompt:
In the Stripe Dashboard, go to Developers → Webhooks. Replace the
endpoint URL https://tessera-staging.up.railway.app/webhook with
https://tesseraboards.com/api/stripe/webhook. Keep the same event list
(checkout.session.completed, invoice.paid, customer.subscription.deleted).
After saving, copy the new signing secret and update STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET
in your Lovable secrets panel. Re-run the test purchase and confirm the
"subscription_status" column flips to "active" in the production
Supabase table within a few seconds.
findings.title: Integrations: Stripe webhook still points at the staging URL
findings.what_we_saw:
The Stripe Dashboard webhook endpoint registered for this account is
https://tessera-staging.up.railway.app/webhook, not your production
domain. The "Sign up" → checkout flow on the live site completes
successfully, but the webhook event fires into the staging environment.
That's why the production database has no record of the test purchase
Mira ran on May 18.
findings.why_it_matters:
Every Stripe-driven side effect (account upgrade, plan flag, welcome
email, license key issuance) lives behind that webhook. Right now no
paid customer is being marked as paid in the production database. They
are charged successfully but stuck on the free plan.

findings.fix_prompt:
Verify your sending domain in Resend (Domains → Add → tesseraboards.com).
Wait for DNS to propagate (usually a few minutes). Then update the
from-address in your /api/reset route from "[email redacted]"
to "[email redacted]" so it matches the verified domain.
Add a try/catch around the Resend call so failures bubble up as a 500
with a clear error toast instead of a misleading 200 + success message.
Test by clicking "Forgot password?" and confirming the email lands.
findings.title: Integrations: password reset email never sends (silently)
findings.what_we_saw:
On the sign-in page, "Forgot password?" shows a confirmation toast ("Check your
email") immediately after submit, but no email arrives at the test
address (verified across two providers, 10 minutes elapsed, spam
checked). Network tab shows the form POSTs to /api/reset, which returns
200 OK. Resend's logs (visible from your dashboard if you check)
indicate the API call fails with "from address not verified."
findings.why_it_matters:
Users who forget their password have no path to recovery. They will
either give up or email support — both outcomes are worse than
necessary, and the silent failure mode means you don't know it's
happening unless someone complains.

findings.fix_prompt:
Wrap the Skip-login button, the role picker modal, and all "for dev"
copy in `if (import.meta.env.DEV) { ... }`. Verify it disappears on
the published Lovable URL by reloading the sign-in page in an incognito tab.
Never gate this by URL, environment variable in the client bundle, or
a feature flag — those can leak.
findings.title: Dev role bypass still visible on the sign-in page in production
findings.what_we_saw:
On the sign-in page, below the standard email/password fields, there's a "Skip
login (dev)" button that opens a role picker (Admin / Member / Viewer)
and signs visitors in without credentials. Helper text reads "For
development purposes only."
findings.why_it_matters:
Anyone with your URL can pick "Admin" and skip authentication entirely
on a paid SaaS. This is the single most dangerous thing on the site
right now — fix before any other work.

findings.fix_prompt:
Pick one initialization site and remove the other. Recommended: keep
the Lovable-managed PostHog provider (set the project key in Lovable
→ Integrations → PostHog), and remove the manual posthog.init call
from _app.tsx. Confirm only one PostHog snippet renders in the page
source. Then go to PostHog → Project settings → Events and delete the
duplicate `signup_completed` events from the last 7 days, or note the
cutover date and treat all pre-cutover numbers as 2x.
findings.title: Integrations: PostHog double-counts every signup event
findings.what_we_saw:
PostHog shows two `signup_completed` events for every user we
registered (timestamps ~150 ms apart, identical distinct_id). The page
source includes the PostHog snippet twice: once in <head> via the
Lovable-managed analytics provider, and once in /pages/_app.tsx via a
`posthog.init(...)` call you appear to have added manually.
findings.why_it_matters:
Every analytics number on your dashboard is currently 2x reality.
Conversion rates and funnel charts will be wrong, and A/B tests will
reach false significance. The kind of thing that bites quietly for
months.

findings.fix_prompt:
Replace `G-XXXXXXXX` in the gtag snippet with your real GA4
measurement ID (Admin → Data streams → Web → "Measurement ID",
starts with G-). After deploying, open the live site, then check
GA4 → Reports → Realtime — you should appear within a minute. If you
don't have a GA4 property yet, create one in
analytics.google.com first.
findings.title: Integrations: GA4 measurement ID is the placeholder G-XXXXXXXX
findings.what_we_saw:
View source on / shows the Google Analytics gtag snippet with
`gtag('config', 'G-XXXXXXXX')`. No data has been received in your
GA4 property (verified via the realtime view).
findings.why_it_matters:
You think you have GA4 data; you don't. If anyone is judging the
launch by GA4 numbers, they'll see zero traffic and panic. Easy fix
and high impact on whoever's reading the dashboards.

findings.fix_prompt:
Add a server-side membership check at the top of the boards lookup
route. In Supabase, the simplest approach is a row-level security
policy on the `boards` table: only return rows where
`auth.uid() in (select user_id from board_members where board_id =
boards.id)`. Then update the page to show a "You don't have access to
this board" empty state when the lookup returns 0 rows, instead of
crashing or showing partial data. Re-run the cross-user test from the
audit before marking this fixed.
findings.title: Cross-user data leak on /board/:id when account A views account B's URL
findings.what_we_saw:
Logged in as test-user-A, we directly visited a board URL we obtained
from test-user-B's account (your site).
The page rendered B's full board (cards, comments, attachment names)
without any 403 or empty state. The API call to /api/boards/87a1c4
returns the row regardless of which user makes the request.
findings.why_it_matters:
Anyone who guesses or shares a board ID can read another customer's
private boards. The IDs aren't easily guessable, but a former
collaborator who leaves a team would still have the URLs in their
history. This is the cross-user leak Scale Up and Pro tiers
specifically test for.

findings.fix_prompt:
Add the privacy page and the terms page routes with basic policy content (you can
adapt LaunchLook's own at launchlook.app/privacy, launchlook.app/terms
as a starting template — replace the company name and support
address). Link both from the footer. Use [email redacted] (or
your real support email) in the contact section.
findings.title: Missing the privacy page and the terms page (404)
findings.what_we_saw:
Both the privacy page and the terms page return the custom 404 page. The footer links
to them but they don't load. Pricing page checkout requests them
indirectly (Stripe surfaces "Terms" links it expects to be valid).
findings.why_it_matters:
Tessera collects board content, account data, and runs subscriptions.
Visitors and Stripe both expect legal pages. Missing them also makes
the site look unfinished.

findings.fix_prompt:
Wire each CTA to the correct Stripe price ID. In your /api/checkout
route, switch on the `plan` query parameter (starter | pro | team)
and use the matching `price_xxx` from your .env. Test all three
buttons with the Stripe test card 4242 4242 4242 4242 and confirm
the Stripe checkout shows the right amount before paying.
findings.title: Pricing page CTAs all open the same Stripe checkout (Pro plan)
findings.what_we_saw:
The pricing page shows three plans (Starter / Pro / Team), but
clicking "Start free trial" on Starter, Pro, and Team all open the
same Stripe checkout page, which charges $19/month for the Pro plan.
Network tab confirms all three buttons hit /api/checkout?plan=pro.
findings.why_it_matters:
Customers who pick Starter or Team are charged Pro pricing. This is
a refund-and-trust-killer, and the kind of bug that goes undetected
until someone complains.

findings.fix_prompt:
In Resend, add `tesseraboards.app` as a verified domain (or switch
everything to the canonical `tesseraboards.com` and verify that).
Add the SPF, DKIM, and DMARC DNS records Resend prompts for. Wait
for the dashboard to flip to "Verified" (usually a few minutes), then
change your sender from `[email redacted]` to a verified
address. Re-test with a fresh Gmail address.
findings.title: Sign-up emails land in Gmail spam folder
findings.what_we_saw:
Test sign-ups using a freshly created Gmail address consistently land
the welcome email in spam (verified across three test addresses).
The email body is fine; the headers show no SPF / DKIM alignment with
the sender domain (`From: [email redacted]`, but DKIM is
signed by `resend.dev`).
findings.why_it_matters:
Activation rate is bottlenecked by users finding their welcome email.
If most signups are on Gmail (likely), most users will think the
email never arrived and bounce.

findings.fix_prompt:
Search for "Your Company Name" in your codebase and replace with
"Tessera Boards" (or your final brand name). Update the year to
2026 while you're there. Re-check the page <title> on the pricing page,
the privacy page, the terms page — those are usually the forgotten three.
findings.title: Footer still says "© 2024 Your Company Name"
findings.what_we_saw:
The footer copyright reads "© 2024 Your Company Name". Same line
appears on every page. The page <title> on the pricing page also reads
"Your Company Name | Pricing".
findings.why_it_matters:
Reads as unfinished to anyone deciding whether to trust the
checkout. Two-second visual cue that the team didn't proof the
published version.

findings.fix_prompt:
Bump card icon-button hit area to 44 × 44 px on screens below 768 px.
Easiest is `min-h-[44px] min-w-[44px] p-2` on the icon button
wrapper, leaving the visual icon size unchanged. Add 8 px gap
between icons so adjacent buttons don't share an edge.
findings.title: Mobile board view: card edit buttons are ~28 px tall (below tap target)
findings.what_we_saw:
On a 375 px wide viewport, the per-card "Edit" and "Move" icons
measure 28 × 28 px and sit 4 px apart. Mistaps on the wrong card are
easy and the modal opens for the wrong item.
findings.why_it_matters:
iOS Human Interface Guidelines and Material Design both recommend
44 × 44 px minimum tap targets. Below that, mistaps are common,
especially on long boards. Mobile usage of Kanban boards skews high
(people review on phones in line at coffee shops).

findings.fix_prompt:
Add an empty-state component to /boards that shows a one-line
heading ("No boards yet"), a short description ("Create your first
board to start organizing your team's work"), and a primary CTA
("Create your first board") that opens the new-board modal. Match
the visual style of the rest of the app (border-line, bg-paper).
findings.title: Empty board state shows nothing — no hint what to do
findings.what_we_saw:
A brand-new account, after sign-up, lands on /boards with a literal
blank screen (no header, no CTA, no copy). The user has no idea
whether the page is loading, broken, or empty.
findings.why_it_matters:
Day-one drop-off is highest right after sign-up. A blank screen
reads as "this product is broken" even when the underlying state
(no boards yet) is correct.

findings.fix_prompt:
In your /index page <head>, add OG meta tags pointing at a custom
OG image you upload to /public/og.png (1200 × 630 px is standard).
The image should show the Tessera logo, a one-line tagline, and
a screenshot or illustration of a board. Re-paste the URL into
Slack to confirm the new preview renders.
findings.title: OG image for / is the Lovable default (gradient with "Lovable")
findings.what_we_saw:
Pasting your site into Slack shows a generic
Lovable gradient with the word "Lovable" instead of a Tessera-
branded preview. Same on iMessage and LinkedIn.
findings.why_it_matters:
Every share is a free marketing impression. With the default OG
image, those impressions look like Lovable's, not yours.

findings.fix_prompt:
Filter the activity feed to exclude users with email domains
matching @tesseraboards-internal.test or names matching common
test patterns. Easier and more durable: add an `is_internal`
boolean to the users table, set it for all team members, and
filter on it in the activity query.
findings.title: Settings page reveals two test accounts in the "Recent activity" feed
findings.what_we_saw:
On /settings → Activity, the feed shows entries from accounts named
"QA Bot 1" and "Internal Tester" creating boards yesterday. These
are visible to any signed-in user.
findings.why_it_matters:
Real users will wonder who those accounts are. It signals that the
product is still in test mode.

share_metadata.description: Pre-launch audit. Verdict: Do not invite real users yet. 14 findings across trust, broken CTAs, mobile layout, and more.
share_metadata.title: LaunchLook audit for Tessera Boards

verdict.label: Do not invite real users yet
verdict.narrative:
Tessera Boards looks ready at a glance: clean Kanban layout, working sign-up,
Stripe checkout that actually completes, and mobile screens that don't break.
The depth review surfaced four operational integration gaps that aren't
visible from the app itself but will bite the moment you turn on paid
traffic: a Stripe webhook pointing at a sandbox URL, an analytics tag that
double-counts every signup, an email sender that lands in spam on Gmail,
and a password reset flow that appears to work but never actually sends
the email. Plus the usual polish layer (placeholder copy, missing trust
pages, mobile tap targets). Fix the integrations before the launch push;
the rest can pace through this week.
verdict.summary: Dev login bypass and a cross-user data leak need to clear before any real traffic.
